params.text:
Near misses are free warnings

It is tempting to ignore a near miss because “nothing actually happened”. That is exactly the wrong response. A near miss is a warning that a real accident is waiting to happen. Reporting it costs nothing but can prevent serious harm.

When near misses are reported, managers can:

  • Spot patterns — for example, repeated slips near the same doorway.
  • Fix the underlying cause before someone is injured.
  • Share the lesson with the whole team.

Services that record lots of near misses are usually safer, not more dangerous — it shows staff feel able to speak up.

params.question: A near miss does not need to be reported because no one was actually harmed.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Near misses are important warnings and should always be reported, even though no one was hurt.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Near misses must be reported. They warn us of hazards so we can act before real harm occurs.
